idea.hook: What's smaller than New York City but weighs more than our Sun?
facts: Manhattan Island stretches approx. 21.6 km (13.4 miles) from end to end.
A typical neutron star is only about 20 km (12.4 miles) in diameter.
Yet, this city-sized sphere contains 1.4 to 2 times the mass of our ENTIRE Sun!
idea.payoff: That's like fitting *two Suns* into an object smaller than Manhattan. The universe's most extreme density!
idea.title: NYC vs. Neutron Star! 🤯

scenes.text:
Smaller than NYC,
Heavier than our Sun?
scenes.visual: Dark cosmic background with subtle twinkling stars. A large question mark icon appears and slowly pulsates.
Overhead map view of Manhattan Island, with a prominent red line indicating its length.

scenes.text:
A Neutron Star:
Only ~20 km (12.4 mi) in diameter
scenes.visual: Manhattan map slides off to the left. A small, incredibly dense-looking, glowing sphere (the neutron star) slides in from the right, appearing to be roughly the same scale as Manhattan's length.

scenes.text:
Contains 1.4-2x the mass
of our ENTIRE Sun!
scenes.visual: The small neutron star remains. A giant, fiery silhouette of the Sun appears next to it, with an arrow pointing from the Sun to the tiny neutron star, and a text overlay 'x2 Mass'.

scenes.text:
That's 2 Suns in a city-sized sphere.
THE UNIVERSE'S MOST EXTREME DENSITY!
scenes.visual: An illustrative graphic showing an outline of Manhattan, with two miniature Sun icons packed tightly inside it. The background darkens, and the text 'THE UNIVERSE'S MOST EXTREME DENSITY!' dominates the screen.
